title: Solar immersion diverter: how it works and whether it pays
subtitle: Routing surplus solar export to a hot water cylinder instead of the grid
excerpt: A solar immersion diverter monitors the household export signal and diverts any surplus generation to an immersion heater instead of feeding it to the grid at 4-15p/kWh. The hot water tank stores it at the equivalent of 27p/kWh. Payback on the diverter unit itself is typically one to two years.
sourceNotes: Marlec / myenergi product documentation (Eddi, iBoost). Energy Saving Trust solar PV and diverters guidance. OFGEM Smart Export Guarantee rates data.

glossaryTerms.definition: Photovoltaic solar panels — convert sunlight directly to DC electricity, then via an inverter to AC for household use.
Kilowatt-hour. A unit of energy equal to one kilowatt of power used (or generated) for one hour. UK electricity is billed in kWh at around 27p/kWh (2025/26 cap).

content.text: system without a battery or diverter exports any generation above household consumption to the grid. The Smart Export Guarantee pays 4-15p per

for that export. A solar diverter intercepts that export and sends it to an immersion heater instead, saving the electricity that would otherwise heat the water at 27p/kWh. The arbitrage is 12-23p per kWh diverted.

The diverter unit installs at the consumer unit and uses a clamp sensor on the grid import/export cable. It continuously measures the balance between generation and consumption. When generation exceeds consumption, the unit applies a proportional power signal to the immersion element, from 0 to 3 kW, to absorb exactly the surplus without drawing from the grid. The result is that surplus generation heats the hot water cylinder rather than trickling back to the grid at a low export rate.
A solar PV system already generating.
A hot water cylinder with an immersion heater element (a combi boiler has no cylinder, a diverter has nothing to heat).
A diverter unit: Eddi (myenergi), iBoost (Marlec), or similar. Units cost supply-only.
A Part P registered electrician to carry out the installation, the unit connects to the consumer unit and the immersion circuit. This is not DIY work.

attrs.title: Part P requirement
attrs.body: Connecting any device to a consumer unit is notifiable electrical work under Part P of the Building Regulations. It must be carried out by a Part P registered electrician who issues a Building Regulations Compliance Certificate. An unregistered installation will not be covered by buildings insurance and may void the warranty on the diverter unit.

content.text: A 4 kWp system in the Midlands exports approximately 1,800-2,200 kWh/year (about half its generation). Diverting all of that to hot water saves: 2,000 kWh × 27p = in electricity avoided, minus the SEG income foregone (2,000 × 10p = ). Net annual benefit: approximately . At a total installed cost of , payback is under two years for most households.

The calculation changes if you are on a high-rate SEG tariff (15p+), the export income foregone is higher, and the case for diversion is weaker. It also requires a cylinder large enough to absorb the surplus: a 150 L cylinder can store roughly 7 kWh as heat, which is sufficient for most summer days.
